title: In Honour Of That High And Mighty Princess, Queen Elizabeth
content:
Proem.
آ آ آ آ Although great Queen, thou now in silence lie,
آ آ آ آ Yet thy loud Herald Fame, doth to the sky
آ آ آ آ Thy wondrous worth proclaim, in every clime,
آ آ آ آ And so has vowï؟½d, whilst there is world or time.
آ آ آ آ So greatï؟½s thy glory, and thine excellence,
آ آ آ آ The sound thereof raps every human sense
آ آ آ آ That men account it no impiety
آ آ آ آ To say thou wert a fleshly Deity.
آ آ آ آ Thousands bring offï؟½rings (though out of date)
آ آ آ آ Thy world of honours to accumulate.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Mongst hundred Hecatombs of roaring Verse,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Mine bleating stands before thy royal Hearse.
آ آ آ آ Thou never didst, nor canst thou now disdain,
آ آ آ آ Tï؟½ accept the tribute of a loyal Brain.
آ آ آ آ Thy clemency did yerst esteem as much
آ آ آ آ The acclamations of the poor, as rich,
آ آ آ آ Which makes me deem, my rudeness is no wrong,
آ آ آ آ Though I resound thy greatness ï؟½mongst the throng.

The Poem.
آ آ آ آ No Phï؟½nix Pen, nor Spenserï؟½s Poetry,
آ آ آ آ No Speedï؟½s, nor Camdenï؟½s learned History;
آ آ آ آ Elizaï؟½s works, wars, praise, can eï؟½re compact,
آ آ آ آ The Worldï؟½s the Theater where she did act.
آ آ آ آ No memories, nor volumes can contain,
آ آ آ آ The nine Olympï؟½ades of her happy reign,
آ آ آ آ Who was so good, so just, so learnï؟½d, so wise,
آ آ آ آ From all the Kings on earth she won the prize.
آ آ آ آ Nor say I more than truly is her due.
آ آ آ آ Millions will testify that this is true.
آ آ آ آ She hath wipï؟½d off thï؟½ aspersion of her Sex,
آ آ آ آ That women wisdom lack to play the Rex.
آ آ آ آ Spainï؟½s Monarch saï؟½s not so, not yet his Host:
آ آ آ آ She taught them better manners to their cost.
آ آ آ آ The Salic Law had not in force now been,
آ آ آ آ If France had ever hopï؟½d for such a Queen.
آ آ آ آ But can you Doctors now this point dispute,
آ آ آ آ Sheï؟½s argument enough to make you mute,
آ آ آ آ Since first the Sun did run, his neï؟½er runnï؟½d race,
آ آ آ آ And earth had twice a year, a new old face;
آ آ آ آ Since time was time, and man unmanly man,
آ آ آ آ Come shew me such a Phï؟½nix if you can.
آ آ آ آ Was ever people better rulï؟½d than hers?
آ آ آ آ Was ever Land more happy, freed from stirs?
آ آ آ آ Did ever wealth in England so abound?
آ آ آ آ Her Victories in foreign Coasts resound?
آ آ آ آ Ships more invincible than Spainï؟½s, her foe
آ آ آ آ She rackï؟½t, she sackï؟½d, she sunk his Armadoe.
آ آ آ آ Her stately Troops advancï؟½d to Lisbonï؟½s wall,
آ آ آ آ Don Anthony inï؟½s right for to install.
آ آ آ آ She frankly helpï؟½d Franksï؟½ (brave) distressed King,
آ آ آ آ The States united now her fame do sing.
آ آ آ آ She their Protectrix was, they well do know,
آ آ آ آ Unto our dread Virago, what they owe.
آ آ آ آ Her Nobles sacrificï؟½d their noble blood,
آ آ آ آ Nor men, nor coin she shapï؟½d, to do them good.
آ آ آ آ The rude untamed Irish she did quell,
آ آ آ آ And Tiron bound, before her picture fell.
آ آ آ آ Had ever Prince such Counsellors as she?
آ آ آ آ Her self Minerva causï؟½d them so to be.
آ آ آ آ Such Soldiers, and such Captains never seen,
آ آ آ آ As were the subjects of our (Pallas) Queen:
آ آ آ آ Her Sea-men through all straits the world did round,
آ آ آ آ Terra incognitï؟½ might know her sound.
آ آ آ آ Her Drake came laded home with Spanish gold,
آ آ آ آ Her Essex took Cadiz, their Herculean hold.
آ آ آ آ But time would fail me, so my wit would too,
آ آ آ آ To tell of half she did, or she could do.
آ آ آ آ Semiramis to her is but obscure;
آ آ آ آ More infamy than fame she did procure.
آ آ آ آ She placï؟½d her glory but on Babelï؟½s walls,
آ آ آ آ Worldï؟½s wonder for a time, but yet it falls.
آ آ آ آ Fierce Tomris (Cirusï؟½ Heads-man, Sythiansï؟½ Queen)
آ آ آ آ Had put her Harness off, had she but seen
آ آ آ آ Our Amazon iï؟½ thï؟½ Camp at Tilbury,
آ آ آ آ (Judging all valour, and all Majesty)
آ آ آ آ Within that Princess to have residence,
آ آ آ آ And prostrate yielded to her Excellence.
آ آ آ آ Dido first Foundress of proud Carthage walls
آ آ آ آ (Who living consummates her Funerals),
آ آ آ آ A great Eliza, but comparï؟½d with ours,
آ آ آ آ How vanisheth her glory, wealth, and powers.
آ آ آ آ Proud profuse Cleopatra, whose wrong name,
آ آ آ آ Instead of glory, provï؟½d her Countryï؟½s shame:
آ آ آ آ Of her what worth in Storyï؟½s to be seen,
آ آ آ آ But that she was a rich ï؟½gyptian Queen.
آ آ آ آ Zenobia, potent Empress of the East,
آ آ آ آ And of all these without compare the best
آ آ آ آ (Whom none but great Aurelius could quell)
آ آ آ آ Yet for our Queen is no fit parallel:
آ آ آ آ She was a Phï؟½nix Queen, so shall she be,
آ آ آ آ Her ashes not revivï؟½d more Phï؟½nix she.
آ آ آ آ Her personal perfections, who would tell,
آ آ آ آ Must dip his Pen iï؟½ thï؟½ Heliconian Well,
آ آ آ آ Which I may not, my pride doth but aspire
آ آ آ آ To read what others write and then admire.
آ آ آ آ Now say, have women worth, or have they none?
آ آ آ آ Or had they some, but with our Queen isï؟½t gone?
آ آ آ آ Nay Masculines, you have thus taxï؟½d us long,
آ آ آ آ But she, though dead, will vindicate our wrong.
آ آ آ آ Let such as say our sex is void of reason
آ آ آ آ Know ï؟½tis a slander now, but once was treason.
آ آ آ آ But happy England, which had such a Queen,
آ آ آ آ O happy, happy, had those days still been,
آ آ آ آ But happiness lies in a higher sphere.
آ آ آ آ Then wonder not, Eliza moves not here.
آ آ آ آ Full fraught with honour, riches, and with days,
آ آ آ آ She set, she set, like Titan in his rays.
آ آ آ آ No more shall rise or set such glorious Sun,
آ آ آ آ Until the heavenï؟½s great revolution:
آ آ آ آ If then new things, their old form must retain,
آ آ آ آ Eliza shall rule Albian once again.

Her Epitaph.
آ آ آ آ Here sleeps T H E Queen, this is the royal bed
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½ thï؟½ Damask Rose, sprung from the white and red,
آ آ آ آ Whose sweet perfume fills the all-filling air,
آ آ آ آ This Rose is withered, once so lovely fair:
آ آ آ آ On neither tree did grow such Rose before,
آ آ آ آ The greater was our gain, our loss the more.

Another.
آ آ آ آ Here lies the pride of Queens, pattern of Kings:
آ آ آ آ So blaze it fame, hereï؟½s feathers for thy wings.
آ آ آ آ Here lies the envyï؟½d, yet unparallelï؟½d Prince,
آ آ آ آ Whose living virtues speak (though dead long since).
آ آ آ آ If many worlds, as that fantastic framed,
آ آ آ آ In every one, be her great glory famed.
meaning: About: Anne Bradstreet was the first notable American poet and the first woman to have her works published in Colonial America.
